titleStmt.title: Letter from William Tyrrell Jr. to William Tyrrell Sr., 5 November 1917
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: William Tyrrell Jr.

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p:
The letters of William Upton Tyrrell ('Willie') to his father William J.H. Tyrrell, mother Elizabeth, sister Minnie and brother Robert are a notable example of wartime correspondence. The letters - nearly 200 in total - are mostly written by William to his family while in active service with the Royal Irish Rifles in France. Willie discusses his life and the lives of other soldiers in the trenches, as well as life at Ballindoolin, the family estate.
The family name 'Tyrrell' is sometimes misspelled 'Tyrell' in correspondence to the family.
In the present letter, Willie says that he received four parcels. Included were rubber boots, apples, pears, cakes, and some moldy pheasants. Twenty parcels were delivered to his regiment at the one time.

msIdentifier.repository: OPW-Maynooth University Archive & Research Centre at Castletown

correspAction.placeName: Ballindoolin House, Ballindoolin, Edenderry, Kings Co., Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
5th/11/17 My dear Daddy, Just after I had sealed up
my letter yesterday the post
came bringing your letter of 31st
and four parcels. One containing rubber
boots. One containing muffles and a
delicious box of apples and pears,
which were in perfect condition.
Not a particle squashed or spoiled
and beautifully ripe. Each of the
other two contained a pheasant but
they were mouldy but did not smell
bad. The cakes and other things
in the parcel were absolutely alright. I do not know how long they
had been on the journey as the
postmarks were unreadable.
One postmark I think was 20 Oct.
Everything was quite alright
except the pheasants and I
hardly expected them to be good. This is particularly bad weather
for sending them as here
is is muggy and close and
things go bad in no time.
I am sure you will be glad
to here they have arrived
alright. There were about
20 parcels in the post last night. I am sure the pastman was saying
mad things. By this day fortnight I
expect I may be near Garrett.
I hope so anyway. By the
time you get this letter we will
be out of the trenches again
+ in nice comfortable billets. The book and magazines in the
parcels were particularly acceptable
as I had nothing to read and
nothing else to do.
Now that I have my boots I am
of fortified on every end and side
against any weather that may
come. Today is bright and
sunny. The first time the sun
came out since we have been i
n the line - + it has made everyone
more cheerful. No news. Best love to all
from Your most affec Son Willie
